examples.text: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ
examples.english: O Mankind! It is you who are in dire need of God; but God is Free of all needs, the Most Worthy of all praise!

body.text: A hollow that is dug around the shoot,
offset, of a palm-tree, when it is planted:
offset, of a palm-tree is planted,
then alluvial soil with dung of camels or the like is pressed down around it:
, with two dammehs: (K, TA:) or this [app. the pl., but accord. to the TA the sing.,] signifies
soft, place, in which wells are dug forming a regular series.
is expl. by A 'Obeyd as meaning
The share of the sons of such a one of the wells.
(S, * O, * K, * TA:) pl. as above. (TA.) ―         -b4-  And it is said to signify
trunk of a palm-tree, by means of which one ascends to an upper chamber:
but the word commonly known in this sense is
. (IAth, TA.)       -A2-  As an epithet applied to a camel, it means
in the manner explained in the first paragraph of this art.;
. (K, TA.)       -A3-  Also, applied to a man, (TA,)
Having the vertebræ of the back broken;
having a complaint of the vertebræ of his back, arising from fracture
having his vertebræ pulled out from his back, so that his spine is interrupted:
having a complaint of his vertebræ:
having the vertebræ of his back broken
(Msb.)       -A4-  Hence, as though having the vertebræ of his back broken, (IDrst, TA in art.
,) [but said to be irregularly formed from
those who dwell with him and whose maintenance is incumbent an him:
who finds food sufficient to sustain life:
who possesses only what is sufficient for life:
, which signifies one who possesses nothing; altogether destitute: (Aboo-'Amr Ibn-El-'Alà, ISk, S, O, K:) or both mean
(IAar, S, O:) Aboo-Haneefeh holds the opinion of ISk, (TA,) who cites the following verse from a poem of Er-Rá'ee in praise of 'Abd-El-Melik Ibn-Marwán;

A^am~aA Alfaqiyru Al~a*iY kaAnato Haluwbatuhu
                      
                     
                        wafoqa AlEiyaAli falamo yutorako lahu sabadu
whose milch camel was sufficient for his household, and nothing
] (S, O, TA:) As says that the
is better in condition than the
is better in condition than the
; and adds, I asked an Arab of the desert, Art thou
? and he answered, No, by God, but rather
: (S, O, TA:) or the former signifies
and the latter, one abased by need or want, or otherwise; (Ibn-'Arafeh, O, K;) who, if abased by need or want, may lawfully receive of the poor-rate; but if abased otherwise than by
need or want, he may not receive of the poorrate; for he may be rich: (Ibn-'Arafeh:) [
i. e., of God's help, &c., and
the needer of the mercy of God,
are epithets which a man often writes before his name:] it is said in the Kur [xxxv. 16],

body.text_bw: AlfuqaraA='u A_ilaY A@ll~`hi waA@ll~`hu huwa A@loganiY~u A@loHamiydu
body.text_ar: الفُقَرَا=ءُ اـِلَى ا@للّٰهِ وَا@للّٰهُ هُوَ ا@لْغَنِىُّ ا@لْحَمِيدُ

body.text: they who stand in need, of God:
and God, He is the Self-sufficient, the Praised in every case:
] (O, * TA: [see also the Kur xxviii. 24:]) or
deprived of the power of motion, by disease,
who suffers from a protracted disease, being weak, and who has no trade;
who has a mean trade that does not suffice for his need;
, a beggar, who has a trade that stands in some stead, (

,) but does not cause him and his household to be without want; (Esh-Sháfi'ee, T, O, K;) so that the former is in a harder condition than the latter accord. to Esh-Sháfi'ee; (T;) and it seems that he is called

because of crippleness, or protracted disease, which prevents his freely employing himself in making gain: (Khálid Ibn-Yezeed:) As also says that the latter is in a better condition than the former; (S, O, K;) and so says Ahmad Ibn-'Obeyd: (TA:) and as to the verse of Er-Rá'ee, cited above, it is said to mean that the person there mentioned had a milch camel in former times, but possessed it no longer, and that

means that nothing was left to him: (Mgh:) the pl. of the latter epithet is also applied in the Kur xviii. 78 to men possessing a ship, or boat, which is worth a considerable sum; (Mgh;) whence Aboo-Bekr holds the opinion of As to be correct: (TA:) but it is urged in reply, that these men were hirers, not owners, of the vessel, as appears from one reading, [app.
,] with teshdeed: (TA:) or the former signifies one
who has neither property nor gain that suffices for his need;
and the latter, one who has property or gain not sufficient for him: or, as some say, the converse is the truth: (Bd in ix. 60:) or both signify the same, (IAar, S, K,) one
(IAar, S:) or when they are used together, they differ in signification; and when used separately, they both [sometimes] signify the same: (El-Bedr El-Karáfee:) [see more voce
(Lh, Msb, TA) like the masc., [said to be] the only instance of the kind except
, and perhaps some other instances, should be added;] but ISd says, I know not how this is. (TA.)
